card.intuition: A complete binary tree where each node stores an aggregate (sum, min, max) over a subarray — a range query decomposes into O(log n) non-overlapping stored segments.
card.analogy: A company org chart where each manager's file summarises all reports below them. To get the total headcount of any division, look up just the relevant manager nodes — no individual counting needed.
card.time_complexity: O(n) build; O(log n) point update; O(log n) range query
card.space_complexity: O(4n) for the tree array — size 4n is safe for any n
when_to_use: Range sum/min/max queries with point updates — the sweet spot over prefix sums (which don't support updates)
Range update + range query with lazy propagation
Counting inversions, range GCD, range OR/AND, range multiply
gotchas: Allocate tree array of size 4n — a tree of n leaves needs at most 4n nodes in 1-indexed representation
Node i's left child is 2i, right child is 2i+1 (1-indexed) — this is the standard segment tree layout
Lazy propagation is required for range updates — without it, a range update takes O(n) not O(log n)

exercise.prompt: Complete the segment tree range sum query. Decompose [l, r] into stored segments.
hints: When the query range completely covers the current segment [start, end], return self.tree[node] — no need to go deeper
Otherwise merge left and right recursive results: return left + right
exercise.test_code:
arr = [1,3,5,7,9,11]
st  = SegmentTree(arr)
assert st.query(0,2)  == 9,   'sum [0,2] = 1+3+5'
assert st.query(0,5)  == 36,  'sum all'
assert st.query(3,5)  == 27,  'sum [3,5] = 7+9+11'
assert st.query(1,1)  == 3,   'single element'
st.update(1, 10)
assert st.query(0,2)  == 16,  'after update: 1+10+5'
assert st.query(0,5)  == 43,  'full sum after update'
print('PASS')
